titleStmt.title: Onsager Theory of Turbulence, the Josephson-Anderson Relation, and the D'Alembert Paradox

analytic.title: Onsager Theory of Turbulence, the Josephson-Anderson Relation, and the D'Alembert Paradox

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The Josephson-Anderson relation, valid for the incompressible Navier-Stokes solutions which describe flow around a solid body, instantaneously equates the power dissipated by drag to the flux of vorticity across the flow lines of the potential Euler solution considered by d'Alembert. Its derivation involves a decomposition of the velocity field into this background potential-flow field and a solenoidal field corresponding to the rotational wake behind the body, with the flux term describing transfer from the interaction energy between the two fields and into kinetic energy of the rotational flow. We establish the validity of the Josephson-Anderson relation for the weak solutions of the Euler equations obtained in the zero-viscosity limit, with one transfer term due to inviscid vorticity flux and the other due to a viscous skin-friction anomaly. Furthermore, we establish weak forms of the local balance equations for both interaction and rotational energies. We define nonlinear spatial fluxes of these energies and show that the asymptotic flux of interaction energy to the wall equals the anomalous skin-friction term in the Josephson-Anderson relation. However, when the Euler solution satisfies suitably the no-flow-through condition at the wall, then the anomalous term vanishes. In this case, we can show also that the asymptotic flux of rotational energy to the wall must vanish and we obtain in the rotational wake the Onsager-Duchon-Robert relation between viscous dissipation anomaly and inertial dissipation due to scale-cascade. In this way we establish a precise connection between the Josephson-Anderson relation and the Onsager theory of turbulence, and we provide a novel resolution of the d'Alembert paradox.

1. Introduction 1.1. Josephson-Anderson Relation for Flow Around a Solid Body. The Josephson-Anderson relation was first derived for voltage-drops in superconductors [15] and chemical potential differences in superfluids [1], relating these to transverse motion of quantized vortices. A "detailed relation" of Huggins [13] further connected superfluid dissipation to flux of vortices across streamlines of the background potential flow and was applied by Huggins also to classical turbulent channel flow described by the incompressible Navier-Stokes equation [14].

Recently, the detailed Josephson-Anderson relation was extended to incompressible Navier-Stokes solutions describing flow around a smooth, solid body [12]. This result made a direct connection with the classical paradox raised by work of d'Alembert [4,5], who showed that the potential Euler solution u φ = ∇φ for flow around the body B predicts no drag. See Fig. 1 for the context. Following the earlier work on superfluids, [12] introduced a "rotational velocity" u ν ω := u νu φ which accounts for all flow vorticity ω ν = ∇ × u ν and which satisfies the equation
div.formula: ∂ t u ν ω = u ν × ω ν -ν∇ × ω ν -∇ p ν ω + 1 2 |u ν ω | 2 + u ν ω • u φ , = -∇ • (u ν ω ⊗ u ν ω + u ν ω ⊗ u φ + u φ ⊗ u ν ω ) -∇p ν ω -ν∇ × ω ν ,(1)
div.p: expressing local conservation of the "vortex momentum" P ν ω = ρ Ω u ν ω dV. Here the pressure p ν ω is to be determined by the divergence-free constraint ∇ • u ν ω = 0 and ρ is mass density of the fluid. The formulation (1) of the incompressible Navier-Stokes equation gives a precise mathematical description of the rotational wake that develops behind the body. In this setting, the Josephson-Anderson relation follows by considering balance equations for the interaction energy between potential and rotational flow, E ν int = ρ Ω u ν ω • u φ dV, and the kinetic energy in the rotational flow itself, E ν ω = (ρ/2) Ω |u ν ω | 2 dV, that is:
div.formula: ∂ t (u φ • u ν ω ) + ∇ • p ν ω + 1 2 |u ν ω | 2 + u ν ω • u φ u φ + p φ + 1 2 |u φ | 2 u ν ω = +u φ • (u ν × ω ν -ν∇ × ω ν )(2)
div.p: and
div.formula: ∂ t 1 2 |u ν ω | 2 + ∇ • p ν ω + 1 2 |u ν ω | 2 + u ν ω • u φ u ν ω -νu ν × ω ν = -u φ • (u ν × ω ν -ν∇ × ω ν ) -ν|ω ν | 2 .(3)
div.p: Clearly, the space-integrated expression
div.formula: T ν = -ρ Ω u φ • (u ν × ω ν -ν∇ × ω ν ) dV(4)
div.p: represents a transfer from the interaction energy to the energy of rotational flow, which is thereafter disposed by viscosity, and it can be interpreted physically in terms of the flux of vorticity across the streamlines of the potential flow [12,13]. This quantity has furthermore a remarkable instantaneous relation to the drag force of the fluid acting on the body: where viscous skin friction or wall stress is given by τ ν w = 2νS ν n = νω ν × n, n is the unit normal on ∂B pointing into Ω, and S ν ij = (1/2)(∂ i u ν j + ∂ j u ν i ) is the strain-rate tensor. The Josephson-Anderson relation derived in [12] states that the transfer ( 4) is equal to D ν := F ν • V, the power consumption from drag, instantaneously in time:
div.formula: F ν = ρ ∂B (-p ν ω n + τ ν w )dA(5)
D ν = -ρ Ω u φ • (u ν × ω ν -ν∇ × ω ν )dV = -ρ Ω ∇u φ : u ν ω ⊗ u ν ω dV + ρ ∂Ω u φ • τ ν w dA(6)
div.p: The expression for T ν in the second line is obtained from the first by simple vector calculus identities and spatial integration by parts. As already remarked in [12], the latter form of the Josephson-Anderson relation should be valid even for weak Euler solutions u obtained as a zero-viscosity limit of the Navier-Stokes solution u ν . It is the purpose of the present paper to derive relation (6) rigorously in the limit ν → 0, under reasonable hypotheses which are sufficient for a weak Euler solution to exist [8]. This approach is obviously connected with the "ideal turbulence" theory of Onsager [20], who posited that infinite Reynolds-number turbulent flows will be described by weak Euler solutions dissipating kinetic energy. For reviews, see [6,11]. In particular, we shall follow the approach of Duchon & Robert [9] to show that the local energy balance equations ( 2), (3) remain valid in the distributional sense for the inviscid limit. The sink term Q ν (u ν ) = ν|ω ν | 2 which appears in Eq.( 3) and which describes the viscous dissipation in the rotational wake behind the body will be shown to converge to a non-negative distribution Q(u) which corresponds to "inertial energy dissipation" or nonlinear energy cascade in the Euler solution. We thus derive a precise connection in the inviscid limit between the Josephson-Anderson relation and Onsager's dissipative anomaly.

1.2. Prior Work. Our analysis here will depend essentially upon the approach and results in our preceding paper [21], which studied the conditions necessary for a momentum anomaly to exist in wall-bounded turbulence, a possibility conjectured already by Taylor [24]. As remarked at the end of [21], all of the main results of that work carry over to the local balance of vortex momentum given by Eq. (1). As there, we assume that those equations admit strong solutions for arbitrarily large Reynolds numbers. Then, Theorem 1 of [21] implies in this context that distributional limits of the wall shear stress τ ν w and the rotational pressure stress p ν ω,w n at the wall exist for ν → 0 :
div.formula: τ ν w ν→0 ---→ τ w in D ((∂B) T , T (∂B) T )(7)
p ν ω n ν→0 ---→ p ω,w n in D ((∂B) T , N (∂B) T )(8)
div.p: More precisely, these limits exist as distributional sections of the tangent and normal bundles, respectively, of the space-time manifold (∂B) T = ∂B × (0, T ), where we assume that B ⊂ R 3 is closed, bounded, and connected, the complement Ω = R 3 \B is also connected, and the common boundary ∂Ω = ∂B is a C ∞ manifold embedded in R 3 . See [21], section 2 for our notations and conventions regarding distribution theory on manifolds. The limit results ( 7),( 8) require hypotheses regarding the strong convergence (u ν ω , p ν ω ) → (u ω , p ω ) as ν → 0, given precisely in the Theorem 1 below, and the limits are then weak solutions of the inviscid version of Eq.( 1):
div.formula: ∂ t u ω = -∇ • (u ω ⊗ u ω + u ω ⊗ u φ + u φ ⊗ u ω ) -∇p ω , ∇ • u ω = 0.(9)
div.p: The second main result of [21] had to do with the spatial cascade of momentum and its matching to stress at the wall. In the present context, the spatial momentum-flux or stress consists of the advective contribution:
div.formula: T ω : = u ω ⊗ u ω + u ω ⊗ u φ + u φ ⊗ u ω (10
div.p: and the isotropic pressure contribution p ω I. A turbulent cascade of rotational momentum is defined for the infinite-Re Euler solution by introducing fields Tω, = G * T ω , pω, = G * p ω spatially coarse-grained at length-scale with a standard mollifier G and a smooth window function η h, (x) = θ h, (d(x)) which =0 when distance to the boundary d(x) < h for h > . The turbulent flux of momentum toward the wall is thus defined by the quantity
To justify this physical interpretation it is important to note that for sufficiently small < η(Ω), for Ω := {x ∈ Ω : d(x) < }, and for any x ∈ Ω , there exists a unique point π(x) ∈ ∂Ω such that

Thus, ∇η h, (x) = θ h, (d(x))n(π(x)) is the product of an approximate delta function θ h, (d(x)) δ(d(x) -h) and the wall normal vector n(x) := n(π(x)) extended into the interior, so that (11) indeed measures momentum transfer toward the wall at distance h. See section 2 in [21] for a detailed discussion of these results. Note also for later use that the unit normal vector n, projection map π and distance function d all belong to C ∞ ( Ω ) for each < η(Ω). With this background, Theorem 2 of [21] states that the wall-parallel component of the flux (11) converges in the limit < h → 0 to the wall tangential stress τ w and that the wall-normal component converges to the wall pressure stress -p ω,w n. For a precise statement, one must be able to interpret (11) as a sectional distribution of the tangent bundle and also of the normal bundle. For this purpose, [21] introduced a non-standard space of test functions

which are non-vanishing on ∂Ω, and also sets E T and E N of extension operators where Ext ∈ E T is a continuous, linear map Ext : Ext * (∇η h, • Tω, + pω, ∇η h, ) = τ w in D ((∂B) T , T (∂B) T ) (14) and for all
div.formula: Ext ∈ E N -lim h, →0 Ext * (∇η h, • Tω, + pω, ∇η h, ) = -p ω,w n in D ((∂B) T , N (∂B) T ). (15
div.p: The final main result of [21] was that, under some additional tenable hypotheses, the advective flux of vortex momentum to the wall must vanish. The added assumptions were, for some > 0, the near-wall boundedness property
with Ω := {x ∈ Ω : dist(x, ∂B) < } and the no-flow-through condition
Then Theorem 3 of [21] implies that, for all Ext ∈ ẼT ,
div.formula: -lim h, →0 Ext * (∇η h, • Tω, ) = τ w = 0 in D ((∂B) T , T (∂B) T )(18)
div.p: and for all Ext ∈ E N ,
div.formula: -lim h, →0 Ext * (p ω, ∇η h, ) = -p ω,w n, in D ((∂B) T , N (∂B) T ). (19
div.p: Here ẼT is a class of natural extensions which consists of those
div.formula: Ext ∈ E T such that ∀ψ ∈ D((∂B) T , T * (∂B) T ), φ = Ext(ψ) satisfies φ • n L ∞ ((Ω h+ \Ω h )×(0,T )) = O( ), h, → 0,(20)
div.p: which allows to infer that pω, ∇η h, gives vanishing contribution to the tangential wall stress in (14), as would be naturally expected. It follows then from (18) that there is no viscous momentum anomaly, τ w = 0, so that all drag originates asymptotically from pressure forces and, furthermore, from (19) the pressure of the Euler solution away from the wall matches onto the pressure at the wall.

The present work will build on these these results for vortex momentum and extend them to rotational kinetic energy and to interaction energy of potential and rotational flow, obtaining the infinite Reynolds-number limit of the Josephson-Anderson relation in the process. Because kinetic energies are scalar quantities, we can simplify somewhat the approach of [21]. Following [25], we denote by D((∂B) T ) the C ∞ sections with compact support of the trivial scalar bundle (∂B) T × R and by D ((∂B) T ) the sectional distributions of that bundle. We refer to these simply as spaces of scalar test functions and of scalar distributions on (∂B) T , respectively. We then define the class of scalar extensions E as the set of continuous, linear maps Ext : ψ ∈ D((∂B) T ) → φ ∈ D( Ω×(0, T )) such that the restriction φ| (∂B) T = ψ. This set E is defined more precisely in the following section 2, where it is shown also that E = ∅ by construction of a concrete example. We shall need further in our proof the fact that the stationary Euler solution describing potential flow around the body B with asymptotic velocity V at infinity satisfies u φ ∈ C ∞ ( Ω), which follows from the known smoothness of the solution of the Laplace equation with zero Neumann conditions on a domain Ω with smooth boundary ∂Ω. We shall review these results also in section 2. Since u φ • n = 0 on ∂B and since ∂B is compact, it follows that we may interpret u φ | ∂B ∈ D((∂B) T , T * (∂B) T ). Thus, the dot product with the distribution τ w ∈ D ((∂B) T , T (∂B) T ) obtained by Theorem 1 of [21] can be defined with
div.formula: u φ • τ w ∈ D ((∂B) T ) by setting u φ • τ w , ψ := τ w , ψu φ | ∂B , ∀ψ ∈ D((∂B) T ).(21)
div.p: 1.3. Results on Interaction Energy and Josephson-Anderson Relation. We can now state the first main result of the present work, which is analogous to Theorem 1 of [21] for vortex momentum, but here on the infinite Reynolds-number limits of the local balance of the interaction energy (2) and of the Josephson-Anderson relation ( 6):

Theorem 1. We make the following assumptions (which are same as those in Theorem 1 of [21]): Let (u ν ω , p ν ω ) be strong solutions of Eq.( 1) on Ω × (0, T ) for ν > 0. Assume that (u ν ω ) ν>0 strongly converges to u ω in L 2 ((0, T ), L 2 loc (Ω)) :
and that (p ν ω ) ν>0 strongly converges to p ω in L 1 ((0, T ), L 1 loc (Ω)) :
Assume furthermore for some > 0 arbitrarily small
div.formula: u ν ω uniformly bounded in L 2 ((0, T ), L 2 (Ω ))(24)
p ν ω uniformly bounded in L 1 ((0, T ), L 1 (Ω )). (25
div.p: Then, the limit (u ω , p ω ) is a weak solution of (9) and for this same sequence and for all Ext ∈ E,
div.formula: lim ν→0 Ext * [u φ • ν∇ × ω ν ] = u φ • τ w in D ((∂B) T )(26)
div.p: Furthermore, the local balance Eq.( 2) for the interaction energy holds distributionally in the sense that for all ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )), with ψ = ϕ| ∂B ,
div.formula: - T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ (u ω • u φ ) dV dt - T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • (u ω • u φ )u + 1 2 |u φ | 2 u ω + p ω u φ + p φ u ω dV dt = -u φ • τ w , ψ + T 0 Ω ϕ (∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω ) dV dt (27) Finally, if (22) is strengthened to global L 2 convergence u ν ω ν→0 ----------→ L 2 ((0,T ),L 2 (Ω)) u ω ,(28)
div.p: then the Josephson-Anderson relation (6) holds also in the inviscid limit, in the sense that
div.formula: D = lim ν→0 F ν • V exists in D ((0, T )) with D, χ = -ρ Ω χ∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω dV dt + ρ u φ • τ w , χ ⊗ 1 (29
div.p: for all χ ∈ D((0, T )).

Remark 1. Although we have so far presented our results by assuming a fluid velocity at infinity V which is time-independent, the proof presented below should allow any function V(t) which is C ∞ in time, corresponding to smooth translational motion of the body B. In that case the potential Euler solution u φ is of course also time-dependent. See footnote [91] in [12] and section 2.2 below for more discussion. It would be interesting to generalize these results further to allow for solid-body dynamics of B including possible rotation, as in [23].

Remark 2. Just as in [21], Theorem 1, condition (23) on convergence of pressure can be replaced with any assumption guaranteeing that along a suitable subsequence of ν, p ν ω → p ω ∈ L 1 ((0, T ), L 1 loc (Ω)) in the sense of distributions. E.g.
p ν ω uniformly bounded in L q ((0, T ), L q loc (Ω)) for some q > 1 (30) would suffice. See [21], Remark 2.

Our next result is analogous to Theorem 2 in [21], which involved spatial momentum cascade, but considering now the spatial flux of interaction energy. Similar to the definition (11) of momentum flux, we apply spatial coarse-graining and windowing to Eq.( 1) for u ν ω and to the Euler equation for u φ , yielding the balance for interaction energy in length scales > and wall distances > h :
div.formula: ∂ t (η h, ūν ω, • ūφ, ) + ∇ • (η h, Jν φ, ) = ∇η h, • Jν φ, + η h, ūν ω, ūν ω, : ∇ū φ,(31)
+ η h, ∇ū φ, : (τ (u ν ω , u ν ω ) + τ (u φ , u ν ω ) + τ (u ν ω , u φ )) -η h, ūν ω, • (∇ • τ (u φ , u φ )).
div.p: Here τ (f, g) := (f g) -f ḡ for any functions f, g on Ω := {x ∈ Ω : d(x) > } and spatial flux of interaction energy is given by Jν
div.formula: φ, = (ū ν ω, • ūφ, )ū ν + pν ω, ūφ, + 1 2 |ū φ, | 2 + pφ, ūν ω, -ν ūφ, × ων + (τ (u ν ω , u ν ω ) + τ (u φ , u ν ω ) + τ (u ν ω , u φ )) • ūφ,(32)
div.p: The balance equation (31) holds also for the limiting fields (u ω , p ω ) of Theorem 1, as stated by the following Proposition, whose easy proof is left to the reader:
Proposition 1. Under the assumptions ( 22)-( 23) of Theorem 1, the following equation holds pointwise for x ∈ Ω and distributionally for t ∈ (0, T ):
div.formula: ∂ t (η h, ūω, • ūφ, ) + ∇ • (η h, Jφ, ) = ∇η h, • Jφ, + η h, ūω, ūω, : ∇ū φ, +η h, ∇ū φ, : (τ (u ω , u ω ) + τ (u φ , u ω ) + τ (u ω , u φ )) -η h, ūω, • (∇ • τ (u φ , u φ )). (33
div.p: where Jφ, is given by the formula (32) with ν → 0, (u ν ω , p ν ω ) → (u ω , p ω ).
Our second main result is then:
Theorem 2. Assume conditions ( 22)-( 25) in Theorem 1. Then
div.formula: -lim h, →0 Ext * [∇η h, • Jφ, ] = u φ • τ w in D ((∂B) T )(34)
div.p: for all Ext ∈ E.

This theorem states that the spatial cascade of interaction energy to the wall equals the skin friction term in the inviscid Josephson-Anderson relation (29). Finally, Theorem 3 of [21] showed that momentum cascade to the wall must vanish when the limiting Euler solution satisfies the no-flow-through condition at the wall in a suitable sense. An exact analogue of this result for interaction energy is given by the following: Theorem 3. Let (u ω , p ω ) be the weak solution of Eq.( 9) from Theorem 1, with
for some > 0. Assume further the stronger near-wall boundedness property
and the no-flow-through condition at the boundary in the sense
Then, for all
div.formula: Ext ∈ E lim h, →0 Ext * [∇η h, • Jφ, ] = 0 in D ((∂B) T ) (37)
div.p: so that u φ • τ w = 0. Thus, an inviscid form of the balance Eq.( 2) for the interaction energy holds distributionally, in the sense that for all ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T ))
div.formula: - T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ (u ω • u φ ) dV dt (38) - T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • (u ω • u φ )u + 1 2 |u φ | 2 u ω + p ω u φ + p φ u ω dV dt = T 0 Ω ϕ (∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω ) dV dt.
div.p: If convergence to the inviscid limit holds in the global L 2 -sense (28), then the Josephson-Anderson relation (6) is valid in the inviscid form
div.formula: D = -ρ Ω ∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω dV. (39
div.p: The implication is that cascade of interaction energy to the wall must vanish if the strong near-wall boundedness and no-flow-through conditions are satisfied.

1.4. Results on Energy Balance of the Rotational Flow. We next wish to show that results hold analogous to those of Duchon & Robert on turbulent kinetic energy balance [9], but now for the energy in the rotational wake. We have:
div.formula: Theorem 4. Let (u ν ω , p ν ω ) be strong solutions of equations (1) on Ω × (0, T ). Assume that (u ν ω ) ν>0 strongly converges to u ω in L 3 ((0, T ), L 3 loc (Ω)), u ν ω ν→0 -----------→ L 3 ((0,T ),L 3 loc (Ω)) u ω ,(40)
div.p: and
div.formula: p ν ω uniformly bounded in L 3 2 ((0, T ), L 3 2 loc (Ω)). (41
div.p: Assume further for some > 0
div.formula: u ν ω uniformly bounded in L 3 ((0, T ), L 3 (Ω ))(42)
p ν ω uniformly bounded in L 3 2 ((0, T ), L 3 2 (Ω )). (43
div.p: Then, along a suitable subsequence
div.formula: p ν ω → p ω ∈ L 3 2 ((0, T ), L 3 2 loc (Ω)) distribu- tionally, so that (u ω , p ω ) is a weak solution of the inviscid equation (9) satisfying the results (26),(27) of Theorem 1. Also, Q ν = ν|ω ν | 2 converges for this sub- sequence to a positive linear functional Q on D( Ω × (0, T )), in the sense that ∀ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )), lim ν→0 T 0 Ω ϕ Q ν dV dt = Q, ϕ(44)
div.p: with Q, ϕ ≥ 0 for ϕ ≥ 0. Finally, an inviscid version of the balance equation (3) for rotational energy holds in the sense that for all ϕ ∈ D( Ω ×(0, T )), ψ = ϕ| ∂B ,
div.formula: - T 0 Ω 1 2 ∂ t ϕ|u ω | 2 dV dt - T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • 1 2 |u ω | 2 u + p ω u ω dV dt = u φ • τ w , ψ -Q, ϕ - T 0 Ω ϕ∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω dV dt(45)
div.p: Remark 3. The result (44) is analogous to Proposition 4 of Duchon-Robert [9], which showed that the inviscid limit of viscous dissipation exists as a nonnegative distribution. In fact, the linear functional Q in our theorem restricted to the subspace D(Ω × (0, T )) is a non-negative distribution. Thus, by standard representation theory for distributions ( [2], Example 12.5; [22], Exercise 6.4), there is a non-negative Radon measure on Ω × (0, T ), which we shall also write as Q by an abuse of notation, so that

We have not shown that a Radon measure Q exists on Ω × (0, T ) such that the representation (46) holds for all ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )), but it is plausible to conjecture that this is true. If so, another natural question is whether Q((∂B) T ) > 0 or = 0. The latter question is related to the famous theorem of Kato [16,23], who showed that, if a smooth Euler solution u exists over the time interval (0, T ), then
div.formula: u ν → u in L 2 ((0, T ), L 2 (Ω)) when u ν 0 → u 0 in L 2 (Ω) and Q ν (Ω cν × (0, T ))
div.p: → 0 for some constant c > 0. Under these specific assumptions, a dissipative Euler solution obtained in the inviscid limit plausibly must have Q((∂B) T ) > 0.

Remark 4. Under the stronger assumptions of Theorem 4 then also the conclusions of Theorem 1 hold, so that one can combine Eq.( 27) of that theorem with the present result Eq.( 45) to obtain the combined balance:
div.formula: - T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ (u ω • u φ ) + 1 2 |u ω | 2 dV dt - T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • (u ω • u φ ) + 1 2 |u ω | 2 + p ω u + 1 2 |u φ | 2 + p φ u ω dV dt = -Q, ϕ(47)
div.p: This combined quantity was termed the "relative kinetic energy" in [12], because it measures the total energy of the fluid relative to the energy of the potential Euler solution and it is thus locally conserved when Q = 0.

Our next result is a close analogue of Theorem 2 but considering now the spatial flux of rotational flow energy. By applying spatial coarse-graining and windowing to Eq.( 1) for u ν ω , we obtain a balance equation for rotational energy in length scales > and wall distances > h :
div.formula: ∂ t ( 1 2 η h, |ū ν ω, | 2 ) + ∇ • (η h, Jν ω, ) = ∇η h, • Jν ω, -η h, ∇ū φ, : ūν ω, ūν ω, +η h, ∇ū ν ω, : (τ (u ν ω , u ν ω ) + τ (u φ , u ν ω ) + τ (u ν ω , u φ )) -νη h, | ων | 2 (48)
div.p: with spatial flux of rotational energy given by Jν
div.formula: ω, = 1 2 |ū ν ω, | 2 ūν + pω, ūν ω, -ν ūν ω, × ων +(τ (u ν ω , u ν ω ) + τ (u φ , u ν ω ) + τ (u ν ω , u φ )) • ūν ω,(49)
div.p: The balance equation (48) holds also for the limiting fields (u ω , p ω ) of Theorem 4, as stated by the following easy Proposition, with proof left to the reader:
Proposition 2. Under the assumptions (40)-(44) of Theorem 4, the following equation holds pointwise for x ∈ Ω and distributionally for t ∈ (0, T ):
div.formula: ∂ t ( 1 2 η h, |ū ω, | 2 ) + ∇ • (η h, Jω, ) = ∇η h, • Jω, -η h, ∇ū φ, : ūω, ūω, +η h, ∇ū ω, : (τ (u ω , u ω ) + τ (u φ , u ω ) + τ (u ω , u φ )) (50
div.p: where Jω, is given by the formula (49) with ν → 0, (u ν ω , p ν ω ) → (u ω , p ω ). Our next main result then relates the spatial cascade of rotational energy toward the wall and the skin friction term in the inviscid Josephson-Anderson relation (29). However, the statement is more complex than our previous Theorem 2 for interaction energy, because it involves also the quantity η h, ∇ū ω, : τ (u ω , u ω ) which represents scale-cascade of rotational energy and as well the anomalous energy dissipation Q in the rotational wake. Thus, we find: Theorem 5. Let (u ω , p ω ) be the limiting weak solutions of the inviscid equation (9) obtained in Theorem 4. For 0 < < h, and ∀ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )) with
div.formula: ψ = ϕ| ∂B , -lim h, →0 T 0 Ω ϕ ∇η h, • Jω, + η h, ∇ū ω, : τ (u ω , u ω ) dV dt = -u φ • τ w , ψ + Q, ϕ .(51)
div.p: The result (51) is not entirely satisfactory, because one would expect that the first and second terms on the lefthand side equal respectively the first and second terms on the righthand side, and not only when added together. We can obtain this physically more natural statement, but only by assuming conditions under which the first terms of each side of (51) vanish: Theorem 6. Let (u ω , p ω ) be the limiting weak solutions of the inviscid equation (9) obtained in Theorem 4, with u ω ∈ L 3 ((0, T ),
for some > 0. Assume further the stronger near-wall boundedness property
and the no-flow-through condition at the boundary in the sense
Then u φ • τ w = 0 in D ((∂B) T ) and for all Ext ∈ E,
div.formula: lim h, →0 Ext * ∇η h, • Jω, = 0. (54
div.p: In consequence, an inviscid form of the balance equation (3) for rotational energy holds distributionally, in the sense that for all ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )),
div.formula: - T 0 Ω 1 2 ∂ t ϕ |u ω | 2 dV dt - T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • 1 2 |u ω | 2 u + p ω u ω dV dt = -Q, ϕ - T 0 Ω ϕ∇u φ : u ω u ω dV dt(55)
div.p: and furthermore
div.formula: -lim h, →0 T 0 Ω ϕ η h, ∇ū ω, : τ (u ω , u ω ) dV dt = Q, ϕ(56)
div.p: Remark 5. The results (55) and (56) of Theorem 6 correspond exactly to those in Proposition 2 of Duchon & Robert [9], who considered L 3 weak solutions of incompressible Euler equation on a torus and derived a local energy balance containing a term due to "inertial energy dissipation," analogous to the lefthand side of our Eq.( 56). Furthermore, the equality in Eq.( 56) corresponds to the result in Proposition 4 of [9], stating that the "inertial dissipation" coincides with the zero-viscosity limit of the viscous energy dissipation, assuming convergence to the weak Euler solution in strong L 3 sense as the viscosity tends to zero.
Remark 6. Theorem 6 implies a strong-weak uniqueness result for any viscosity weak solution u ω ∈ L ∞ ((0, T ), L 2 (Ω)). In fact, the quantity

coincides with the "relative energy" used by Wiedemann [26] to prove strongweak uniqueness. We do not give full details here, but just sketch main ideas. Under the assumptions of Theorem 4 only we obtain that for a.e. t ∈ (0, T )
div.formula: E ω (t) -E ω (0) ≤ u φ • τ w , 1 - t 0 Ω ∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω dV ds(58)
div.p: This inequality follows from the result (45) of Theorem 4 by substituting ϕ = χ [0,t] χ η U R where χ [0,t] is an -smoothed characteristic function of [0, T ] and χ η U R is an η-smoothed characteristic function of U R = Ω ∩ B(0, R) and by then taking the limits → 0 and R → ∞. Therefore, if we use the consequence u φ • τ w = 0 of Theorem 6, then it easily follows from (58) that
div.formula: E ω (t) ≤ E ω (0) + Cρ t 0 ∇u φ (s) L ∞ (Ω) E ω (s) ds(59)
div.p: and thus by Gronwall inequality
div.formula: E ω (t) ≤ E ω (0) exp Cρ t 0 ∇u φ (s) L ∞ (Ω) ds .(60)
div.p: In particular, we see that if u 0 = u φ , then u(t) = u φ for a.e t ∈ [0, T ]. Note that (58) can be derived also when initial data u ν 0 → u φ strongly in L 2 (Ω), by taking ϕ = χ [0,t] χ η U R in (110) below for ν > 0, and then taking the successive limits → 0, ν → 0, and R → ∞. Thus, u φ • τ w = 0 implies strong-weak uniqueness even if the initial data satisfy u ν 0 = 0 at ∂Ω because of a thin boundary layer.

div.head: Preliminaries
div.p: We present briefly here some background material needed for our proofs.
2.1. Extension operators.. Extension operators of the type discussed in the introduction are defined more precisely by
as linear and continuous operators, satisfying pointwise equality φ| (∂B) T = ψ.
Here Ext is continuous in the sense that for a multi-index α = (α 1 , α 2 , α 3 , α 4 ) with |α| ≤ N , ∀(x, t) ∈ Ω × (0, T )
div.formula: |D α ϕ(x, t)| = |D α Ext(ψ)(x, t)| sup i∈I p N,m,i (ψ)(62)
div.p: where denotes inequality with constant prefactor depending on the domain (∂B) T and the extension operator Ext. Here, (p N,m,i ) are the seminorms associated with the Fréchet space D((∂B) T ), where i ∈ I index a system of smooth charts ∪ i∈I (V i , φ i ), φ i : V i ⊂ (∂B) T → R 3 , and m is the index of a fundamental increasing sequence (K (i) m ) of compact subsets of φ i (V i ). See section 2 in [21]. We denote by E the set of all such linear and continuous operators from D((∂B) T ) to D( Ω × (0, T )). We show that E is non-empty by constructing an extension operator explicitly. For 0 < < η(Ω), we define
by means of the explicit expression
div.formula: ϕ(x, t) = exp -d(x) -d(x) ψ(π(x), t), d(x) < 0 d(x) ≥(64)
div.p: for any 0 < < η(Ω). Ext 0 is clearly linear in ψ by definition. ϕ is smooth by the smoothness of distance function d and projection π. One can easily obtain the bound (62) for Ext 0 by product rule and chain rule in calculus.
We use extension operators to identify F ∈ D ((0, T ), C ∞ ( Ω)) with distributions on the space-time boundary (∂B) T . For each Ext ∈ E, we define
as follows:
div.formula: Ext * (F ), ψ := F, Ext(ψ)(66)
div.p: for all ψ ∈ D((∂B) T ). The linearity and continuity properties of Ext * (F ) follow from those of Ext ∈ E, so that Ext * (F ) ∈ D ((∂B) T ). This identification of functions on the whole domain with distributions in D ((∂B) T ) depends of course on the choice of the extension operator Ext.

div.head: Potential flow solution.
div.p: The potential-flow solution u φ of the incompressible Euler equation is given by u φ = ∇φ for the velocity potential φ which solves the following Neumann problem for the Laplace equation:
allowing for the moment a time-dependent external velocity field V(t) = V (t)x. The Euler pressure p φ is then obtained from the Bernoulli equation

and is unique up to the arbitrary space-independent constant c(t). For our proofs below it is essential that φ ∈ C ∞ ( Ω×(0, T )) and thus possesses smoothness up to the boundary ∂Ω. In the general case of smoothly time-dependent velocity V(t), this property will depend upon differentiability of the solution of the Neumann problem (67) in the boundary data. Here, for simplicity, we shall consider only time-independent flow at infinity, so that the required smoothness follows from the following result:
Proposition 3. The Neumann problem (67) of the Laplace equation for the case
div.formula: V(t) ≡ V has a unique time-independent solution φ ∈ C ∞ ( Ω).
div.p: Proof. This result is essentially classical but we provide a proof here for completeness. By the mapping φ := φ -V x, any solution φ of ( 67) is transformed into a solution φ of the following inhomogeneous Neumann problem:

From classical potential theory, a unique solution φ ∈ C 2 (Ω) ∩ C 1 ( Ω) of (69) exists. See e.g. [17], Theorem 6.10.6; [19], Theorem 2.1. Since φ is harmonic in the open set Ω, necessarily φ ∈ C ∞ (Ω). See e.g. [10]. Thus, φ = φ + V x is also a smooth function in C ∞ (Ω) and in C 1 ( Ω).

It then suffices to show φ is smooth in a neighborhood of the boundary ∂Ω. Since B is compact in R 3 , B B(0, R) for some large R > 0. Define U := B(0, R) ∩ Ω, which has C ∞ smooth boundary ∂U = ∂Ω ∪ ∂B(0, R). For some sufficiently small r, we have Ω 2r ⊂ U . To further localize near the boundary, we consider a smooth non-increasing function θ : R → [0, 1] such that θ = 1 in (-∞, r] and 0 in [2r, +∞). We denote ξ(x) := θ(d(x)). As we reviewed in the introduction, the distance function
div.formula: d is C ∞ because of C ∞ smoothness of ∂Ω. Thus, ξ ∈ C ∞ ( Ω), is supported in Ω 2r and ξ ≡ 1 in Ω r . Since φ| U ∈ C 1 ( Ū ), then ξφ ∈ H 1 (U )
div.p: By definition, f ∈ C ∞ ( Ū ), since φ is C ∞ in Ω and ∆ξ = ∇ξ = 0 in Ωr . Furthermore U f dV = 0. Since ∂U is C ∞ , we can deduce from boundary elliptic regularity theory, such as Theorem 4 in section 4.2 of [18], that
div.formula: φ H k+2 (Ωr) ≤ ξφ H k+2 (U ) f H k (U ) < ∞ (72)
div.p: for all integers k ≥ 1. Here, the inequalities above with constant prefactors depend only on Ω, R, r. The Sobolev embedding theorem implies φ ∈ C k ( Ωr ) for any integer k ≥ 0, and thus φ ∈ C ∞ ( Ωr ). Together with the interior smoothness of φ, we conclude that φ ∈ C ∞ ( Ω).

div.head: Proof of Theorems 1-3
div.p: 3.1. Proof of Theorem 1. We take an arbitrary ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )) and let ψ = ϕ| ∂B . Testing the interaction energy equation ( 2) against ϕ yields
div.formula: - T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ(u ν ω • u φ ) dV dt - T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • (u ν ω • u φ )u ν + 1 2 |u φ | 2 u ν ω + p ν ω u φ + p φ u ν ω dV dt = T 0 Ω ϕ(u φ • ν u ν ) dV dt + T 0 Ω ϕ(∇u φ : u ν ω ⊗ u ν ω ) dV dt(73)
div.p: As ϕ ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )), there exists a compact subset

div.p: which vanishes as ν → 0. By Proposition 3, u φ is smooth everywhere in Ω×(0, T ) and tangential at the boundary ∂B, so that

div.formula: ψu φ | ∂B (x, t) → (x, t, ψu φ | ∂B (x, t), 0). (77

div.p: Combining ( 75)-(78) gives
div.formula: - T 0 Ω ϕu φ • ν u ν dV dt ν→0 ---→ u φ • τ w , ψ .(79)
div.p: Note that for every extension operator Ext ∈ E, we can define

div.p: for all ψ ∈ D((∂B) T ) with ϕ = Ext(ψ) ∈ D( Ω × (0, T )). Thus, as ν → 0

div.p: Just as in Theorem 1 and Lemma 1 of [21], we can deduce from the assumptions (22)(23)(24)(25) that
and as ν → 0
This implies that as ν → 0
div.formula: - T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ(u ν ω • u φ ) dV dt → - T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ(u ω • u φ ) dV dt(84)
div.p: All of the terms in (73) converge along the same sequence as ν → 0 to their inviscid limit, by very similar arguments as above. Thus, we obtain the local balance equation ( 27) for interaction energy. Finally, we can write the Josephson-Anderson relation (6) in the form
div.formula: T 0 dtχ(t)F ν (t) • V dt = -ρ T 0 Ω χ∇u φ : u ν ω ⊗ u ν ω dV dt + ρ u φ • τ ν w , χ ⊗ 1(85)
div.p: after smearing with χ ∈ D((0, T )). Because the velocity potential φ in Proposition 3 is harmonic in R 3 \B, it follows that |∇u φ | = O(|x| -3 ) as |x| → ∞; e.g. see [17], Proposition 2.17.3. Since also φ ∈ C ∞ ( Ω), it holds immediately that ∇u φ ∈ L ∞ ((0, T ) × Ω). Together with assumption (28), we get that as ν → 0

div.p: and the righthand side is well-defined because u ω ∈ L 2 ((0, T ), L 2 (Ω)). Using also the convergence (7) of the skin friction τ ν w , we conclude that
div.formula: lim ν→0 T 0 dtχ(t)F ν (t) • V dt = -ρ T 0 Ω χ∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω dV dt + ρ u φ • τ w , χ ⊗ 1 .

div.p: The convergence of all of the rest of the terms in the equation (110) can be easily deduced by Lemma 1 in [21] in conjunction with the assumptions (40-43), except for the term involving Q ν . We thus deduce from (110) that the limit (44) of Q ν , ϕ must exist as well with
div.formula: Q, ϕ := T 0 Ω ∂ t ϕ • 1 2 |u ω | 2 dV dt T 0 Ω ∇ϕ • 1 2 |u ω | 2 u + p ω u ω dV dt + u φ • τ w , ψ - T 0 Ω ϕ∇u φ : u ω ⊗ u ω dV dt.(115)
div.p: From this definition, the balance equation (45) trivially follows. Furthermore, Q defined by ( 115) is clearly a linear functional on D( Ω × (0, T )). Finally, since Q ν , ϕ ≥ 0 for all ν > 0 when ϕ ≥ 0, then the limit functional also satisfies the corresponding inequality Q, ϕ ≥ 0. Thus, Q is non-negative.
4.2. Proof of Theorem 5. We take an arbitrary ϕ ∈ D( Ω×(0, T )) with ψ = ϕ| ∂B .
Testing the inviscid coarse-grained balance equation (50) for energy in rotational flow against ϕ and rearranging yields

Since the terms considered in (117)-(120) all converge in L 1 ((0, T ), L 1 (K ϕ )), we conclude from (116) that By comparison with (51), we obtain the equality (56) between inertial energy dissipation and zero-viscosity limit of viscous energy dissipation.
